```python
               (max(ref_offset, est_offset) - min(ref_onset, est_onset))).

    The Average Overlap Ratio (AOR) is given the mean OR computed over
    all matching reference and estimate notes.
...
```

In general I agree. I don’t think it makes too much sense for these examples though, because they are not installed with neither the pip nor the AUR package. Their intended audience is not end users, but developers who want to take a look on how to use the aioxmpp API.

I can see some advantages when one has multiple aioxmpp repositories checked out, which is handy while preparing releases.

As a middle way, let’s go with:

```
os.path.join(os.environ.get("XDG_CONFIG_HOME", os.path.expanduser("~/.config")), "aioxmpp_examples.ini")
```

This will work correctly on XDG-compliant distributions, and everyone else gets a more-or-less usable default. That doesn’t hurt, because before there was no default at all, the file is unlikely to exist or contain any surprising content, and one can still override that using the command line option.

Elements in `daynames` are already unique and since it moved to the global namespace the `set` still would be better. It is only used for checking if a date part is contained in this collection (line 45) so for `tuple` this would mean unnecessary iteration because it is still sequence. In case of miss this is a scan over whole tuple content - O(n). Sets in Python allow for fast lookups and efficient membership testing using `in` operator - O(1). See [PythonSpeed](https://wiki.python.org/moin/PythonSpeed) as a reference.

I assume that redundant set creation on every call was reducing the performance but once it is moved to the global scope there is a substantial gain of using it instead of list or tuple. Mutability is not a problem here. Also, you can move further and use its immutable counterpart - `frozenset`. I don't think if it can give any time performance advantage here but for sure it is more memory efficient because it does not require excessive overallocation of underlying hash table structure.

We need to check to see what happens with the ``venv`` module itself. Ideally the ``LegacyBuilder`` will only be concerned with making an environment that matches the behavior of the ``VenvBuilder``. This is extremely empirical. Suppose you start at offset 5, then receive 3 compressed messages. The compressed enclosing message will have offset 8, and the enclosed messages will have offsets (6, 7, 8) in protocol 0, or (0, 1, 2) in protocol 1. ```
```
In [87]: scales = [5,3]
    ...: image= np.random.rand(10, 10)

In [88]: util.view_as_windows(image, (5, 5)).shape
Out[88]: (6, 6, 5, 5)

In [89]: util.view_as_windows(image, (3, 3)).shape
Out[89]: (8, 8, 3, 3)
```
-- gives no. of windows 36 for scale=5 and 64 for scale=3
For smaller scales, the region can't be corresponded with large scales at image border of width _(large_scale - small_scale)_. Now if i do, 
```
In [90]: init = int(0+max(scales)/2 - 3/2)

In [91]: end = int(10-max(scales)/2 + 3/2)

In [92]: util.view_as_windows(image[init:end,init:end], (3, 3)).shape
Out[92]: (6, 6, 3, 3)
```
Now, this will have same corresponding centers with (scale=5)'s windows. 
And these will go in same n_pix column of entropy matrix with diff. rows.

Without taking a deep dive into DSP theory, the number of taps is the number of nonzero coefficients in a discrete-time difference equation. Gaussian filters are symmetric and non-causal, so they always have an odd number of taps inspecting the samples on either side and including zero. A 13-tap filter looks one index farther away from zero at every point compared to an 11-tap filter, and it will yield different results. If done correctly they both approximate an ideal Gaussian, but the 13-tap filter should be a better approximation.

Even though @benjello's point about a lower point is totally valid from an analytical standpoint, I would still recommend `ETERNITY` for consistency with other enum names.

If the other types are `MONTH` and `YEAR`, for example, you want the last one to also be a noun, not an adjective (`PERMANENT`) or an adverb (`ALWAYS`).

This might sound like arguing over nothing at first, but I can tell you it will otherwise always have that little weird feeling when you type it, even though you might not realise why, and your brain will always be slightly longer to retrieve the word (until you're so used to it it doesn't carry any meaning anymore —but API design should target newcomers too :wink:).

>These settings remind of our "derived settings" which caused issues in KA Lite.

Critical difference here: I prefixed `_content_root` with an underscore so that it's not exported -- i.e. it's not a Django setting, and can't be overridden in Django settings files, so we don't end up with the "I changed this and the derived stuff didn't change" confusion.

>It's very nice to define them in a non-configurable way, but having them sit in the django settings module is perhaps not the right place, since it implies that they can be configured? Maybe they can? But should they?

We will not be recommending to regular end users that they ever use Django settings to configure anything. `CONTENT_STORAGE_DIR` and `CONTENT_DATABASE_DIR` are for a specific "power user" case that is already being utilized by us on our demo servers -- having a single shared `CONTENT_STORAGE_DIR` across demo instances, but separate `CONTENT_DATABASE_DIR` directories, so that we can leverage deduplicated content storage while showing different (but sometimes overlapping) content across demo servers.

The wrapper widget uses an initial placeholder update (and delete) URL (stored in the `data-href-template` attribute [1], and looking like `/dashboard/catalogue/attribute-option-group/__fk__/update/`) [2] [3], which is then later modified with an actual primary key, and set as the real URL by the JavaScript code [4], whenever the selected attribute option group changes (whether manually, or when a new one is created in the pop-up window) [5].

And it remains generic by using `django.core.urlresolvers.reverse`, with the URL pattern name constructed from the app label and model name (in this case, `catalogue` and `AttributeOptionGroup` becoming `reverse('dashboard:catalogue-attribute-option-group-update', args=('__fk__',))`. So the regex should also match alphabetic characters, and the underscore character. Django uses the dot metacharacter (`r'^(.+)/change/$'`) for the ModelAdmin urls [6], which is even more permissive. Files generated by tools that the user has elected to use can be ignored by the user like so:

```sh
mkdir -p ~/.config/git
cat ~/.config/git/excludesfile <<EOF
# Created by pytest
.pytest_cache/
EOF
touch ~/.config/git/config
git config --global core.excludesfile ~/.config/git/excludesfile
```

The problem is that artman resolves all filepaths in the config to be relative to the config filepath and assumes that the artman config file is at the highest (or equal) level directory compared to the files in the config. 
For example, in googleapis, for `google/pubsub/artman_pubsub.yaml`, all the pubsub API files are located at or below that directory level, like `google/pubsub/v1/pubsub.proto`.

However, in discovery-artifact-manager, the relevant discovery document is not in the same directory path as the artman config.
For example, for `gapic/google/compute/artman_compute.yaml`, the relevant discovery doc is at `discoveries/compute.v1.json`.

So there are two options:
1. this proposed PR, which allows specifying the base `discovery-artifact-manager` location
2. Removing the check in https://github.com/googleapis/artman/blob/master/artman/config/loader.py#L198, which would allow specifying the discovery_doc path as "../../discoveries/compute.v1.json" relative to the artman config filepath.

So... the reason I suggested checking for `read` or `write` here is that it seems useful to catch mistakes where someone accidentally passes in some non-file-like object. The problem with checking for `close` is that there are lots of objects in Python that have `close` methods but aren't file-like (e.g. sockets, generators, ...), so it will tend to produce false negatives. OTOH I can't think of any case where an object can useful be called "a file" but it doesn't have `read` or `write`. (Technically I guess we don't even *require* `close` since if the user never calls our `close` method then all will be well. Though it is a bit weird that `close` shows up in `dir()` even if the wrapped object doesn't have it.) So I think checking for `read` or `write` (and possibly also `close`, I dunno) would be better?
